_schema_notes: Each question has: id, category, difficulty (easy|medium|hard), prompt, choices (array of 4 strings), answer (0-3 index into choices).
Optional: quip_correct, quip_wrong â€” shown in the modal after answering. If absent, the game falls back to generic quips.
Scoring multipliers by difficulty: easy 1.5x, medium 2.0x, hard 3.0x. Configurable in Scoring.h.
The 'meta' category is for questions about the game itself â€” rules, mechanics, what a stone block does. These are always fair game.
Add as many questions as you like. The loader shuffles at startup and tracks what's been asked this session so repeats are rare.

questions.prompt: Which series uses the Dallara IR-18 as its spec chassis?
questions.quip_correct: Spec chassis, wild racing.

questions.quip_wrong: Don't overthink things, my friend.

questions.prompt: Who is the author of the novel House of Leaves?
questions.quip_correct: Danielewski spent ten years crafting that labyrinth of a book.
questions.quip_wrong: Mark Z. Danielewski published House of Leaves in 2000. His sister is the singer Poe.

questions.prompt: In House of Leaves, what is unusual about the house on Ash Tree Lane?
choices: It exists in two time periods at once
It is larger on the inside than the outside
It slowly rotates throughout the day
It has no doors or windows
questions.quip_correct: Bigger on the inside, and not in a fun TARDIS way.
questions.quip_wrong: The house is bigger on the inside than the outside, with impossible hallways and shifting rooms.

questions.prompt: In House of Leaves, how is the word 'house' uniquely presented throughout the text?
choices: It is always printed in blue
questions.quip_correct: Blue like the void. A small detail that burrows into your brain.
questions.quip_wrong: The word house is always printed in blue, one of the book's most iconic typographic choices.

questions.prompt: What is the name of the fictional documentary within House of Leaves that chronicles the Navidson family?
choices: The Five and a Half Minute Hallway
questions.quip_correct: The Navidson Record, a film that may not exist, about a house that should not.
questions.quip_wrong: It's The Navidson Record. The Whalestoe Letters is a companion piece, and The Five and a Half Minute Hallway is a chapter.

questions.prompt: The book Airframe was written by:
questions.quip_correct: The dev read this when he was in school.
questions.quip_wrong: The dev read this when he was in school.

questions.prompt: A Keith Olbermann skit on SNL boasted that he read Animal Farm by _______ also known as Eric Blair.
questions.quip_correct: The first, when I was only 15.
questions.quip_wrong: When I was equipment manager for the cross country team...

questions.prompt: What year was Windows 3.1 released?
questions.quip_correct: 1992! Back when multitasking meant switching between Notepad and Solitaire.
questions.quip_wrong: It was 1992. The year Aladdin hit theaters and Windows hit desktops.

questions.prompt: Which puzzle game about hidden explosives was first included with Windows 3.1?
questions.quip_correct: Minesweeper! The reason millions learned to right-click.
questions.quip_wrong: It was Minesweeper. Responsible for more lost productivity than email.

questions.prompt: Windows 3.1 required which underlying operating system to run?
questions.quip_correct: MS-DOS! Windows was just a pretty face on top of a command line.
questions.quip_wrong: It was MS-DOS. Windows 3.1 was essentially a graphical shell sitting on top of DOS.

questions.prompt: Which iconic UI element did Windows 95 introduce that still exists today?
questions.quip_correct: The Start button! Over 30 years old and still going strong.
questions.quip_wrong: It was the Start button. The one thing Microsoft tried to kill in Windows 8 and had to bring back.

questions.prompt: Which Rolling Stones song was used in the Windows 95 ad campaign?
questions.quip_correct: Start Me Up! Microsoft reportedly paid $3 million for the rights.
questions.quip_wrong: It was 'Start Me Up.' Get it? Start... button? Microsoft paid millions for that pun.

questions.prompt: What year was Windows 95 released?
questions.quip_correct: 1995! The clue was in the name all along.
questions.quip_wrong: It's right there in the name: 1995.

questions.prompt: During its live demo, Windows 98 embarrassed Microsoft by showing what error?
questions.quip_correct: The Blue Screen of Death! Bill Gates handled it like a champ.
questions.quip_wrong: It was the Blue Screen of Death. Bill Gates was on stage when it happened. The audience loved it.

questions.prompt: What was the updated re-release of Windows 98 called?
questions.quip_correct: Second Edition! Because 'We Fixed It' wouldn't fit on the box.
questions.quip_wrong: It was Windows 98 Second Edition, commonly known as Windows 98 SE.

questions.prompt: Windows 98 was the first Windows to ship with built-in support for which now-universal connector?
questions.quip_correct: USB! Though it still took years before anyone had enough ports.
questions.quip_wrong: It was USB. Windows 98 made plug-and-play a reality... most of the time.

questions.prompt: What does 'ME' stand for in Windows ME?
questions.quip_correct: Millennium Edition! Released in 2000, right on time for the new millennium.
questions.quip_wrong: It was Millennium Edition. Though many users had other words for it.

questions.prompt: Windows ME introduced which home video-editing application?
questions.quip_correct: Windows Movie Maker! The birthplace of a million cringe home videos.
questions.quip_wrong: It was Windows Movie Maker. One of the few good things to come out of Windows ME.

questions.prompt: Windows ME was the last consumer version of Windows built on which codebase?
questions.quip_correct: The MS-DOS/9x kernel! After ME, Microsoft went all-in on the NT architecture.
questions.quip_wrong: It was the MS-DOS/9x kernel. Windows XP moved everyone to the NT codebase.

questions.prompt: What does 'XP' stand for in Windows XP?
questions.quip_correct: eXPerience! Some people refused to leave it for over a decade.
questions.quip_wrong: It was eXPerience. Microsoft wanted you to know computing was going to be fun now.

questions.prompt: What was the name of Windows XP's famous default wallpaper showing rolling green hills?
questions.quip_correct: Bliss! It's a real photo taken in Sonoma County, California.
questions.quip_wrong: It was Bliss. Photographer Charles O'Rear shot it on a real hillside in California.

questions.prompt: What were the two main consumer editions of Windows XP?
questions.quip_correct: Home and Professional! The eternal question: do I need Pro or not?
questions.quip_wrong: It was Home and Professional. A tradition Microsoft has continued ever since.

questions.prompt: What was Windows Vista's development codename?
questions.quip_correct: Longhorn! Named after a bar near Whistler, British Columbia.
questions.quip_wrong: It was Longhorn. The development took so long the codename became more famous than the product.

questions.prompt: What glass-like visual theme did Windows Vista introduce?
questions.quip_correct: Windows Aero! Beautiful to look at, brutal on your GPU.
questions.quip_wrong: It was Windows Aero. Luna was XP's theme. Aero was Vista's shiny new look.

questions.prompt: Which much-criticized Vista security feature constantly asked users for permission?
questions.quip_correct: UAC! Even Apple mocked it in their 'I'm a Mac' ads.
questions.quip_wrong: It was User Account Control. 'Cancel or Allow' became Vista's unofficial motto.

questions.prompt: What Windows 7 feature lets you arrange windows side-by-side by dragging them to screen edges?
questions.quip_correct: Snap! So simple, so useful, so impossible to live without once you know it.
questions.quip_wrong: It was Snap. Drag a window to the edge and it snaps into place.

questions.prompt: What year was Windows 7 released?
questions.quip_correct: 2009! Unlike Windows 95, the name gives you no hint at all.
questions.quip_wrong: It was 2009. The '7' in the name refers to it being the 7th major Windows release.

questions.prompt: Windows 7 was widely praised as a major improvement over which predecessor?
questions.quip_correct: Windows Vista! 7 was basically Vista done right.
questions.quip_wrong: It was Windows Vista. Windows 7 fixed what Vista got wrong and became a fan favorite.

questions.prompt: What tile-based interface replaced the traditional Start menu in Windows 8?
questions.quip_correct: The Start Screen! A bold move that did not go over well.
questions.quip_wrong: It was the Start Screen, featuring the Metro-style live tiles that divided the world.

questions.prompt: Windows 8 controversially removed which classic desktop element?
questions.quip_correct: The Start button! Microsoft brought it back in 8.1 after the backlash.
questions.quip_wrong: It was the Start button. Its removal was so unpopular that third-party Start menus became a booming business.

questions.prompt: What was the name of the app marketplace introduced with Windows 8?
questions.quip_correct: Windows Store! Later rebranded to the Microsoft Store.
questions.quip_wrong: It was the Windows Store. It's now called the Microsoft Store.

questions.prompt: What digital assistant was built into Windows 10?
questions.quip_correct: Cortana! Named after the AI in Halo. She's since been retired from Windows.
questions.quip_wrong: It was Cortana, borrowed from the Halo universe. She's been phased out of Windows since.

questions.prompt: Which web browser did Windows 10 introduce as a replacement for Internet Explorer?
questions.quip_correct: Microsoft Edge! Originally its own engine, now rebuilt on Chromium.
questions.quip_wrong: It was Microsoft Edge. It eventually switched to the Chromium engine under the hood.

questions.prompt: Windows 10 brought back which UI feature that Windows 8 had removed?
questions.quip_correct: The Start menu! The people demanded it, and Microsoft listened.
questions.quip_wrong: It was the Start menu. Windows 10 combined the classic menu with live tiles as a compromise.

questions.prompt: Where did Windows 11 move the taskbar icons by default?
questions.quip_correct: The center! Giving the taskbar a macOS-like look.
questions.quip_wrong: It was the center. You can switch it back to the left in Settings if it bugs you.

questions.prompt: What security chip does Windows 11 require, which many older PCs lack?
questions.quip_correct: TPM 2.0! The requirement that left millions of perfectly good PCs behind.
questions.quip_wrong: It was TPM 2.0 — Trusted Platform Module. It caused a lot of upgrade headaches.

questions.prompt: What type of mobile apps could Windows 11 originally run through a special subsystem?
questions.quip_correct: Android apps! Via the Windows Subsystem for Android, though Microsoft later discontinued it.
questions.quip_wrong: It was Android apps, through the Windows Subsystem for Android. It was later discontinued.

questions.prompt: A conference designed to last from 8 am to 3 pm or so is called a:
questions.quip_correct: Don't ask the dev to get up at 5 am for this!
questions.quip_wrong: Who wants to drive back and forth 2 hours in a day?
